strict_win_deltas.delta: P2 responds with `inserted: importedItems.length`, derived from the array it actually constructed and pushed, while P1 responds with `inserted: req.body.items.length`, coupling the response to the raw input rather than the actual insertion result.

P2 computes `nextId` via `items.reduce(...)` guarded by `typeof item.id === 'number' && Number.isFinite(item.id)`, whereas P1's `Math.max(0, ...items.map((item) => item.id))` yields `NaN` for every new id if any existing item lacks a numeric id, and can throw a stack-size RangeError via spread on a very large `items` array.

As the store grows across repeated imports, P1's spread-based `Math.max(0, ...items.map(item => item.id))` scales worse and is fragile to any malformed existing id, risking the 'distinct numeric id that does not collide' guarantee long-term, while P2's guarded `reduce` computation stays safe under the same growth.

P2 destructures `const { items: batch } = req.body || {};` giving the payload its own name distinct from the module-level `items` store, whereas P1 reuses the bare `items` identifier for the store while also referencing `req.body.items` in the same function (e.g. `Math.max(0, ...items.map(...))`), making it harder to tell which `items` is meant.
